system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — beginners-guide-to-jazz-guitar

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This book is a compact, systematically sequenced beginner's method for jazz guitar organized entirely around a single harmonic vehicle: the **ii V I progression** (Dm7–G7–Cmaj7). Every element of the curriculum—chord vocabulary, comping rhythms, picking hand technique, chromatic harmony, scale shapes, ornaments, and mixed solo texture—is introduced, practiced, and applied within that same progression. Chapter 1 maps the full curriculum in advance, names all components, and establishes the method's meta-protocol: watch video alongside the PDF, review the chapter, practice each section until comfortable, then advance. This iterative-reinforcement-with-deliberate-pacing sequence is not just advice; it is the governing structure that every subsequent chapter re-instantiates in domain-specific form.

The book's pedagogy follows a consistent three-stage arc within each chapter: **isolate → internalize → integrate**. A new technique is introduced in isolation (with metronome), then applied over a backing track, then combined with previously learned material. This structure is explicit in Chapter 2 (solo practice → metronome → backing track → rhythmic variation), Chapter 5 (memorize each scale shape → apply over backing track → combine both shapes), and Chapter 7 (learn solo as written → replace parts → improvise freely). The final chapter's four-stage study sequence—imitate, replace single notes with own lines, keep single notes while creating new chord rhythms, improvise both freely—represents the method's most fully articulated expression of this arc.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The book builds its vocabulary through **named, labeled techniques** that carry precise structural definitions. The two foundational comping rhythms introduced in Chapter 2 exemplify this: the **Freddie Green rhythm** is defined as quarter-note chords on every beat with accents on beats 2 and 4 to imitate swing feel; the **Charleston rhythm** places chords on beat 1 and the & of 2. Each name anchors a specific rhythmic rule, enabling students to master, recall, and recombine them. Chapter 3 extends this logic to the picking hand with **bass-first** and **melody-first** patterns—each a named, functionally distinct approach rather than a generic strumming variation.

Chapter 4 introduces **approach chords** as a chromatic comping system with its own directional terminology: *from above* (one fret above the target shape, then resolve down) and *from below* (one fret below, then resolve up). The mechanical rule is absolute: move the target chord voicing chromatically by exactly one fret, then resolve into it. Chapter 6 introduces **chromatic ornaments** via the **134 pattern**—a four-note ornament triggered by a specific fingering occurring anywhere on the fretboard in any key—governed by a non-negotiable resolution law: the pattern must always complete to the third finger or it sounds unintentional. Both systems share the book's characteristic move of encoding technique as a rule that applies mechanically across contexts, not as a feel-based suggestion.

## Scale System and Fretboard Integration

Chapter 5 establishes the book's soloing system on a single harmonic premise: because all three chords of the ii V I exist within the parent major key, **one major scale covers the entire progression**. The system delivers this through two positional shapes—**Shape 1**, rooted on the 6th string, and **Shape 2**, rooted on the 5th string—each learned separately before being combined to cover the full neck. The method is explicit that the goal is fretboard integration: knowing both shapes unlocks soloing across positions, not just in one fixed location. Scale shapes are positional fingerings anchored to string roots, not abstract patterns, and the chapter's practice arc—memorize shape → apply immediately over backing track → combine shapes—enforces applied improvisation before technical perfection is achieved.

## Rules, Prescriptions, and the Earn-Before-Restraint Philosophy

Several rules recur across the book and operate as inviolable prescriptions rather than stylistic options. **Mandatory resolution** applies in Chapter 4 (approach chords must resolve into the target chord) and Chapter 6 (the 134 pattern must complete to the third finger). **Prescribed learning sequences** govern each chapter: the order of study is not left to the student but is specified—approach from above before below, bass-first before melody-first, Freddie Green before Charleston before mixing. **Self-extension through variation** is taught as a deliberate skill: once mechanics are secure, the student is explicitly instructed to vary rhythm, picking patterns, and directions to develop musical range beyond the exercise as written.

Chapter 6 articulates the book's most distinctive philosophical prescription: **deliberate overplay before restraint**. Students are instructed to consciously overuse the 134 chromatic ornament first, training the ear to hear it fully, and only then pull back for musical integration in solos. This earn-before-restraint principle—you must first saturate your playing with a technique before you can deploy it with taste—encodes an epistemology of acquisition that distinguishes this method from approaches that emphasize immediate stylistic discretion.

## Culminating Method: Mixed Chords and Single Notes

Chapter 7 represents the synthesis endpoint of the book's curriculum, combining harmonic accompaniment (comping) and melodic soloing into a single **mixed solo** texture. This skill is contextualized for real-world performance: in duos or pianoless trios, the guitarist must supply harmonic space without a dedicated chord instrument. The chapter's four-stage study sequence is the book's most granular articulation of its overall method—imitate the written solo, substitute your own single-note lines, retain the single notes while improvising new chord rhythms, then improvise both layers freely over chord changes. This sequence maps the progression from copying to full improvisation through discrete, verifiable intermediate stages, embodying the book's fundamental pedagogical conviction that improvisation is taught by progressively loosening constraints, not by being thrown into open-ended playing from the start.

# Chapter 1 — Beginner's Guide to Jazz Guitar Intro

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This chapter is introductory rather than instructional, establishing the conceptual roadmap and pedagogical framework for the entire jazz guitar method. The course elements overview identifies the core curriculum components—Chord Shapes, Chord Rhythms, Picking Patterns, Chromatic Chords, Major Scales, Chromatic Ornaments, and Mixing Chords and Scales—all presented within the foundational ii V I progression. The recommended study sequence introduces the load-bearing methodology of the text: watch video lessons alongside the PDF, review the initial chapter, practice each section until comfortable, then advance systematically through subsequent chapters. This protocol establishes the characteristic pedagogical approach of the method—iterative reinforcement combined with deliberate pacing—that students will apply throughout their progression through the material.

# Chapter 2 — Chapter 1 — II V I Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

The ii V I progression is presented as the foundational organizing principle of jazz guitar, and this chapter establishes the core chord vocabulary (Dm7, G7, Cmaj7, Cmaj9) with an explicit learning sequence: solo practice, metronome work, backing track integration, then rhythmic variation. Two named comping rhythms form the load-bearing terminology: the Freddie Green rhythm, defined as quarter-note chords on every beat with accents on beats 2 and 4 to imitate swing feel, and the Charleston rhythm, which places chords on beat 1 and the & of 2. The chapter’s characteristic move is establishing reproducible labeled techniques—each rhythm has a named source and specific structural rule—that students can master through isolated practice before combining them. The transition from imitation to improvisation occurs through mixing the Freddie Green and Charleston rhythms together, giving students a compositional tool for developing their own comping rather than playing either rhythm verbatim.

# Chapter 3 — Chapter 2 — Picking Hand Patterns

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

This chapter teaches two foundational picking hand patterns—bass-first and melody-first—that expand a guitarist’s comping vocabulary beyond basic strumming by varying what the picking hand emphasizes rather than changing chord shapes. The bass-first pattern separates bass notes from melody and is essential when playing solo or without a bassist, while the melody-first pattern highlights the top voice and serves chord soloing and chord melody contexts. The master’s characteristic pedagogical move is prescriptive sequencing: learn bass-first, then melody-first, then blend them together as a progressive skill integration. Key load-bearing terms include comping pattern, picking hand approach, and pattern variation and transposition, with the chapter’s core principle that the picking hand—not just chord shapes—is the variable that unlocks advanced accompaniment technique. Practice discipline mirrors this progression: work each pattern as written first, then explore rhythmic variation and transposition once internalized.

# Chapter 4 — Chapter 3 — Approach Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches approach chords, a chromatic comping technique where you play the same chord voicing one fret above or below your target chord, then resolve into it to create tension and release. The foundational rule is simple: approach chords are executed by moving the target shape chromatically up or down one fret, establishing the precise mechanical action on the fretboard. The pedagogical sequence—approach from above, then from below, then mixing both directions—governs how students internalize the technique through deliberate practice stages. A characteristic move in the chapter is the emphasis on coloring simplified lead sheet symbols (reading Cmaj7 but playing Cmaj9, for instance), reflecting the real-life jazz convention where performers add harmonic color to basic chord symbols written on lead sheets. Once the shapes are secure, the chapter explicitly instructs students to vary rhythm and picking patterns to expand the exercise beyond the basic mechanics, teaching self-extension as part of mastery. The load-bearing terminology includes approach chords, resolve, comping, and the directional distinction of from above versus from below.

# Chapter 5 — Chapter 4 — Major Scale Patterns

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

This chapter teaches two major scale shapes—one rooted on the 6th string and one on the 5th string—as the foundation for soloing over ii V I progressions, leveraging the harmonic principle that all three chords exist within the parent major key. The method’s characteristic move is the two-phase practice protocol: memorize each shape with a metronome, then immediately apply it over a backing track before moving to the next shape, emphasizing early applied improvisation over perfect technical mastery. The chapter introduces the key concept of scale shape (positional fingerings anchored to specific string roots) and fretboard integration, teaching students to individually master each shape and then combine them to cover the full neck in their soloing. Load-bearing terminology includes ii V I scale compatibility (the harmonic justification for using one scale across the progression), Shape 1 root and Shape 2 root (the string-based positional anchors), and the progression from isolated scale learning sequence through combining both shapes as the structural arc of acquisition.

# Chapter 6 — Chapter 5 — 134 Pattern

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 teaches chromatic ornaments as the essential bridge from scale knowledge to authentic jazz phrasing, introducing them as structured tension-and-release devices through the 134 fingerpattern. The core mechanism is the 134 pattern—a four-note ornament triggered whenever that fingering appears on any string in any key—which must always resolve completely to the third finger to avoid sounding unintentional. The master’s characteristic move here is pedagogical: deliberate overplay of the new ornament to train the ear, then restraint for musical integration in solos, reflecting an earn-before-restraint philosophy. The chapter’s load-bearing terms are chromatic ornaments (organized chromaticism with resolution), the 134 pattern (the specific fingering trigger), and mandatory pattern resolution (the non-negotiable technical law). Integration is incremental—isolated position work with metronome, then combination of 6th and 5th string shapes, then free application over backing tracks—encoding the book’s progressive method for absorbing new chromatic vocabulary.

# Chapter 7 — Chapter 6 — Chords and Single Notes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

This chapter teaches the technique of mixing chords and single notes within a solo, a skill essential for ensemble playing in duos or pianoless trios where a guitarist must fill harmonic space without a dedicated chord instrument. The master’s approach is pedagogically progressive, employing a four-stage study sequence that moves from imitation to improvisation: first learn the solo as written with metronome and backing track, then replace single notes with your own lines, then keep the single notes fixed while creating new chord rhythms, and finally improvise both chords and single notes freely over the chord changes. The load-bearing terms here are mixed solo (the hybrid texture of chords and melody) and the study sequence itself, which codifies how to internalize this texture through deliberate practice stages rather than all-at-once improvisation.

# Chapter 1 — Beginner's Guide to Jazz Guitar Intro

_Pages 3-3._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. ## Course elements overview

> The main goal of this course is to introduce you to the different elements of
> playing jazz guitar, presented over a ii V | progression.
>
> These elements include:
>
> Chord Shapes
>
> Chord Rhythms
>
> Picking Patterns
> Chromatic Chords
>
> Major Scales
>
> Chromatic Ornaments
> Mixing Chords and Scales
> And more.

**Significance (p. 3).** This passage defines the full curriculum scope of the method — chord shapes, rhythms, picking patterns, chromatic chords, major scales, ornaments, and their integration — establishing the conceptual map students will follow.

## Recommended study sequence

> Watch the video lesson as you follow along with the PDF.
> Go back to the beginning and re-watch the first chapter.
> Work on chapter 1 in the PDF until it’s comfortable.
> Move on and watch the chapter 2 video.
>
> Work on chapter 2 in the PDF until it’s comfortable.
> Repeat for each chapter.

**Significance (p. 3).** This gives the explicit practice protocol — watch, review, drill to comfort, then advance — which is the method's rule for pacing through material.

# Chapter 2 — Chapter 1 — II V I Chords

_Pages 4-8._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## ii V I progression importance

> The ii V lis the most important progression in jazz, and therefore it’s the best
> place to start when learning jazz guitar.

**Significance (p. 4).** This establishes the foundational pedagogical premise of the entire method — that the ii V I is the organizing principle from which all other learning flows.

## ii V I chord shapes

> To begin, here are four chords that fit over the ii V | progression, with the Cmaj7
> and Cmaj9 being two colors of the Cmaj7 sound.
>
> Start by playing these chords on your own, memorize them, and then add in a
> metronome when ready.
>
> From there, get these chords onto the backing track when you can, play it as
> written, then start to play with the rhythms and picking from there.

**Significance (p. 4).** This passage introduces the core chord vocabulary (Dm7, G7, Cmaj7, Cmaj9) and gives the explicit learning sequence: solo practice → metronome → backing track → rhythmic variation.

## Freddie Green rhythm definition

> The first rhythm is called the Freddie Green rhythm, as it was made famous by
> Count Basie guitarist Freddie Green.
>
> The rhythm is all quarter notes, meaning you play one chord per beat.

**Significance (p. 5).** This defines the Freddie Green comping rhythm by name and its structural rule — one chord per beat in quarter notes — giving the student a labeled, reproducible technique.

## Freddie Green accent rule

> To give it a more authentic Freddie sound, accent the 24 and 4" chords/beats in
> each bar.
>
> This imitates the hi-hat and helps you bring a higher level of swing to the rhythm.

**Significance (p. 5).** This is a specific performance rule — accent beats 2 and 4 — that distinguishes authentic swing feel from mere chord-on-every-beat strumming.

## Charleston rhythm definition

> Charleston rhythm, as it’s based on the dance
> from the 1920s.
>
> This rhythm places the first chord on beat 1 of each bar, then the second chord is
> on the & of 2, between beats 2 and 3 in each bar.

**Significance (p. 6).** This defines the Charleston rhythm by name and gives its precise placement rule (beat 1 and the & of 2), making it a teachable, repeatable rhythmic pattern.

## mixing comping rhythms

> From there, mix the Freddie Green and Charleston rhythms together to expand
> on this idea in your comping.

**Significance (p. 6).** This gives the student a compositional rule for developing their own comping — combining the two named rhythms rather than playing either one verbatim — marking the transition from imitation to improvised application.

# Chapter 3 — Chapter 2 — Picking Hand Patterns

_Pages 9-10._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. ## Bass-melody separation pattern

> The first picking exercise separates the bass from the melody.
>
> This type of comping pattern is helpful when there’s no bassist in your group,
> when you play solo guitar, or for technical development.

**Significance (p. 9).** Defines the core purpose and use cases for the bass-first picking pattern, establishing when and why a guitarist would choose this approach.

## Learning order for patterns

> Start by learning the bass first pattern, then melody first pattern, then blend them
> together to take things to the next level in your comping.

**Significance (p. 9).** Prescribes the explicit pedagogical sequence for building comping technique, treating pattern integration as a progressive skill.

## Expanding picking hand approach

> Often times you learn chords and then strum and pluck them without altering the
> picking.
>
> This is leaving a lot of room to explore in your comping by expanding on your
> picking hand approach to these chords.

**Significance (p. 9).** States the core methodological principle of the chapter — that the picking hand, not just chord shapes, is the key variable in advanced comping.

## Melody-first comping uses

> Bringing out the melody is useful for comping, chord soloing, and especially chord
> melody playing.

**Significance (p. 10).** Defines the musical contexts where the melody-first picking pattern applies, connecting the technique to specific jazz guitar styles.

## Pattern variation and transposition

> Work this pattern as written, then play with the rhythms and take it to other keys
> if you’re ready.

**Significance (p. 9).** Gives a two-stage practice rule — first internalise as written, then vary rhythm and transpose — applicable to both exercises in the chapter.

# Chapter 4 — Chapter 3 — Approach Chords

_Pages 11-13._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Approach chord definition

> Approach chords are where you play a chromatic chord one fret above or below
> the target chord you’re aiming for.

**Significance (p. 11).** This is the foundational definition of the chapter's core technique, establishing the precise mechanical rule for what constitutes an approach chord.

## Approach chord tension/release function

> Approach chords bring a tension and release sound to your comping, jazzing up
> your chords along the way.

**Significance (p. 11).** This explains the harmonic purpose of the technique, teaching the player why approach chords work musically, not just how to execute them.

## Approach chord sequence order

> Start with the approach chords from above, then from below, then mix both
> together to take your comping to the next level today.

**Significance (p. 11).** This gives the pedagogical sequence — above, below, combined — which is the structural rule governing how the student should practice and internalize the technique.

## Lead sheet chord simplification rule

> In jazz, you normally see the simplest chord symbol
> written on a lead sheet, then you as the performer color that chord from there. So,
> it’s good to get used to seeing Cmaj7 but playing the Cmaj9 color as it imitates a
> real-life musical situation.

**Significance (p. 11).** This defines a core jazz performance convention — reading simplified symbols and adding color — that governs how players interpret lead sheets throughout the genre.

## Approach from above mechanics

> The first exercise features approach chords from above, where you play the same
> shape one fret higher, then resolve into your target chord from there.

**Significance (p. 12).** This specifies the exact mechanical procedure for above-approach chords: same voicing shape, one fret up, then resolve — the actionable rule the player executes on the fretboard.

## Approach from below direction

> You can also work approach chords from below and resolve into your target chord
> from there in your comping.

**Significance (p. 13).** This establishes the second approach direction, completing the two-sided chromatic approach system introduced in the chapter.

## Mixing approach directions

> When ready, mix this pattern with the approach chords from above to double
> your fun with this workout.

**Significance (p. 13).** This instructs the student to combine both approach directions, which is the synthesis step that makes the technique practically applicable in real comping.

## Rhythm/pattern variation rule

> feel free to change the rhythms and picking patterns when comfortable to
> expand this approach chord exercise even further.

**Significance (p. 13).** This is an explicit instructional rule that rhythmic and picking variation should be applied once the shapes are secure, teaching the student how to self-extend the exercise.

# Chapter 5 — Chapter 4 — Major Scale Patterns

_Pages 14-16._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## ii V I scale compatibility

> Because all 3 chords ina ii V | are in the parent key, so Dm7-G7-Cmaj7 are all in C
> major, you can use the tonic major scale to solo over all of those chords.

**Significance (p. 14).** This is the foundational harmonic rule of the chapter — it justifies using a single scale over all three chords of the ii V I progression, which is the core soloing concept being taught.

## scale learning sequence

> Start by learning these scale shapes one at a time, add them to your soloing
> workout, then combine them to cover the fretboard in your solos.

**Significance (p. 14).** This gives the explicit method for progressing through the material: individual shapes first, then integration across the fretboard — a structured practice prescription.

## Shape 1 practice method

> Start by memorizing the scale shape, then working it with a metronome.
>
> As soon as you’re ready, you know the scale but it’s not perfect, throw on the
> backing track and solo with this scale over the C major ii V I.

**Significance (p. 14).** This defines the two-phase practice protocol — metronome drilling followed by applied improvisation over the backing track — which is the method's characteristic move for internalizing each shape.

## Major Scale Shape 1 root

> here’s the 6*-string C major scale to memorize and add to your solos
> over the backing track.

**Significance (p. 14).** Identifies Shape 1 as rooted on the 6th string, establishing the positional anchor for the first scale fingering.

## Major Scale Shape 2 root

> Here’s the second major scale shape from the 5" string to learn, memorize, work
> with a metronome and solo with over the backing tracks when ready.

**Significance (p. 15).** Identifies Shape 2 as rooted on the 5th string, establishing the positional anchor for the second scale fingering and completing the two-shape system taught in the chapter.

## combining both shapes

> From there, solo over the backing track with this scale shape, then with the 6-
> string shape only, then mix both together.

**Significance (p. 15).** This is the integration instruction that teaches students to eventually combine both shapes across the neck, which is the culminating skill of the chapter.

# Chapter 6 — Chapter 5 — 134 Pattern

_Pages 17-21._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## scales vs. chromatic ornaments

> The next step beyond playing scales is the most essential to bring a jazz sound to
>
> your solos, and that’s adding chromatic ornaments.

**Significance (p. 17).** This establishes the chapter's core pedagogical claim: scale knowledge is necessary but insufficient; chromatic ornamentation is the critical next layer for jazz phrasing.

## chromatic ornaments defined

> Chromatic ornaments bring a tension and release sound to your playing by
> introducing organized chromaticism and resolution in the shape of patterns.

**Significance (p. 17).** This is the book's definition of chromatic ornaments, framing them as structural tension-and-release devices rather than random passing tones.

## 134 pattern application rule

> This pattern is used whenever you have a 134 fingering on any string, any key,
> and in any scale.

**Significance (p. 17).** This rule generalizes the pattern beyond a single scale or position, teaching the student to recognize the fingering trigger as the application condition.

## rhythm freedom in pattern

> The rhythms you use aren’t that important, meaning you have a lot of freedom to
> experiment with rhythms when using the 134 pattern.

**Significance (p. 18).** This explicitly releases the student from rhythmic rigidity, clarifying that rhythmic variation is encouraged so long as the resolution rule is obeyed.

## mandatory pattern resolution

> The essential item is that you resolve the pattern to the 3" finger.
>
> If you leave things hanging and don’t resolve the pattern by playing all 4 notes,
> you sound like you made a mistake.

**Significance (p. 18).** This is the single non-negotiable technical rule of the chapter: incomplete resolution of the 4-note pattern is categorically wrong, not a stylistic choice.

## applying pattern to scale shapes

> Here, you play up the 6" string shape as is, then you add in the 134 pattern on the
> way down on strings 3 and 4, where the 134 fingerings lie in this shape.

**Significance (p. 19).** This specifies exactly where within the 6th-string major scale shape the 134 pattern is inserted, giving precise positional instruction for integration.

## overplaying to internalize ornament

> It can take time to get this new chromatic ornament into your ears, so overplay it
> in the beginning.
>
> When your ears get used to this sound, you can pull back and add the 134 pattern
> here and there in a more musical way in your solos.

**Significance (p. 19).** This is a deliberate pedagogical strategy—deliberate overuse before musical restraint—that reflects the book's method for ear-training new chromatic vocabulary.

## practice sequence for positions

> Work this position with a metronome, then combine it in your technical studies
> with the 6"-string shape.
>
> When ready, solo over the backing track with the 5'-string shape, then the 6*-
> string shape, then both combined.

**Significance (p. 20).** This prescribes the exact incremental practice sequence—isolated position, combined positions, then free application—encoding the book's progressive integration method.

# Chapter 7 — Chapter 6 — Chords and Single Notes

_Pages 22-24._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## mixing chords and single notes

> By learning how to mix chords and single notes in your solos, you’re prepared to
> jam in a duo or pianoless trio down the road.

**Significance (p. 22).** States the core musical rationale for the technique — it fills harmonic space in ensemble contexts without a dedicated chord instrument.

## study sequence for mixed solo

> Start by learning the solo as written with a metronome and then work it over the
> backing track.
>
> When that’s comfortable, replace the single notes with your own soloing lines.
>
> From there, keep the single notes as written and you make up the chords and
> chord rhythms.
>
> Lastly, when comfortable, you replace both the chords and single notes to create
> your own mixed solo over these chord changes.

**Significance (p. 23).** Provides the explicit four-stage progressive practice method for internalizing the mixed chord-and-melody technique, moving from imitation to improvisation.
